The AI That Outresearched Its Own Creator
Karpathy left a script running for 48 hours. It made 700 experiments. It found improvements he had missed after 20 years of doing this manually.
If that sentence didn't make you stop and think, read it again.
Andrej Karpathy
@karpathy
·
Mar 9
Three days ago I left autoresearch tuning nanochat for ~2 days on depth=12 model. It found ~20 changes that improved the validation loss. I tested these changes yesterday and all of them were additive and transferred to larger (depth=24) models. Stacking up all of these changes,

TL;DR (Read This First)

The One-Liner: An AI agent is now doing the bread-and-butter work of ML research, autonomously, overnight, and it's already outperforming one of the best researchers alive.
  • What it is: A closed loop where an AI agent modifies training code, runs 5-minute experiments, keeps what works, discards what doesn't, and repeats forever
  • What it found: 20 real improvements Karpathy missed, stacking to an 11% performance gain that transferred to larger models
  • Who can use it: Anyone with an NVIDIA GPU. The whole codebase is 630 lines. Setup is 10 minutes.
  • The catch: You still have to write good instructions for the agent. That's now the job.

The Setup: What ML Research Actually Looks Like

Most people picture AI researchers as visionaries having eureka moments at whiteboards.
The reality is mostly this:
You have a hunch. You change one number in your code. You wait 30 minutes for training to finish. You check if the result improved. You write a note. You have another hunch. You change another number. You wait again.
Over and over. For weeks. For months. For years.
This is called hyperparameter tuning, and it's the bread and butter of applied ML research. It's how you figure out the right model size, the right learning rate, the right optimizer settings, the right attention window. Hundreds of small knobs that determine whether your neural network trains well or trains like garbage.
It's genuinely important work. Every major architecture improvement of the last decade has this unglamorous process baked in somewhere. But it's also slow, tedious, and completely bottlenecked by the fact that humans need sleep, get bored, and can only run one experiment at a time.
@karpathy
's insight: what if you just stopped doing that part yourself?

The Alpha: What Autoresearch Actually Is

Think of it like this.
Imagine you hire a research assistant. This assistant has read every ML paper ever written, never gets tired, works 24 hours a day, and has one job: try things on your training code, check if results improved, keep the wins.
That's
autoresearch
.
It's a simple loop that runs forever:
  1. Read the research instructions you wrote
  2. Review what's been tried before and what worked
  3. Form a hypothesis ("what if I tried X?")
  4. Change the code
  5. Train for exactly 5 minutes
  6. Check the metric. Did it improve?
  7. Yes: save the change permanently. No: discard it.
  8. Go back to step 1
At 12 experiments per hour, you get roughly 100 experiments overnight. While you're sleeping, watching something, living your life — the agent is quietly stacking improvements.

How It Works: Three Files, One Loop

The whole project lives in a single GitHub repo. Three files that matter. That's it.
prepare.py
— The Referee. Never Touched.
This file sets up the training data, the tokenizer (converts raw text into numbers the model can process), and the evaluation function. It grades the agent's work. The agent can't cheat here. The scoring is fixed.
train.py
— The Agent's Playground.
The single file the agent is allowed to edit. It contains the full neural network: model architecture, optimizer settings, every hyperparameter. Everything is fair game. The agent can change the model's depth, width, how attention works, how gradients flow. As long as the file still runs and reports a score at the end, the agent can do whatever it wants.
program.md — Your Only Job.
A plain English document you write. It tells the agent what it's trying to do, what strategies to use, what to explore first. Think of it as the job description and research philosophy for your automated research team.
The better your program.md, the smarter and faster the agent's research becomes. This is where the human stays in the loop. Not by tuning hyperparameters. By writing better instructions for the thing that tunes hyperparameters.

Why the 5-Minute Rule Is the Most Important Design Decision

Every training run lasts exactly 5 minutes. No exceptions.
This sounds arbitrary. It's not.
If you let runs go to completion, you break the comparison. A model that trains for 2 hours will naturally score better than one that trains for 20 minutes. The agent could "improve" the metric just by making the model smaller so it trains faster, or by tweaking something that only helps early in training.
Fix the time budget and you fix the comparison problem. Every experiment gets the same compute. An improvement means you're genuinely getting more out of the same 5 minutes, which is exactly the kind of improvement that scales when you eventually train for real.
There's a second benefit. The agent optimizes specifically for your hardware. An H100 processes far more in 5 minutes than an RTX 3080. The agent's solution ends up tailored to your exact machine. That's actually what you want.

What Else Can You Point This At?

The loop doesn't care about neural networks. It cares about three things: a number that goes up or down, a file an agent can edit, and experiments that finish fast enough to run hundreds of them.
Once you see that, the applications get interesting quickly.
Finance & Trading
  • Strategy hyperparameter search — Your quant strategy has settings you've tested maybe 50 combinations of. The agent tests 1,000 in a weekend.
  • Portfolio allocation tuning — Risk weights, rebalancing thresholds, hedging ratios. Sharpe ratio as the fitness function.
  • DeFi protocol parameters — Collateral ratios, interest rate curves, liquidation thresholds. Faster than governance.
  • MEV bot optimization — Gas bidding strategies, bundle ordering logic. Profit per block as the metric.
Science & Research
  • Drug candidate screening — Molecular structure parameters against a binding affinity proxy. Runs in simulation, not a lab.
  • Materials discovery — Alloy compositions, polymer structures. Any domain with a fast computational proxy metric.
  • Climate model calibration — Model parameters against historical data. The agent handles what took graduate students months.
  • Protein folding parameters — Folding algorithm settings against structure prediction accuracy scores.
Product & Growth
  • A/B test automation — Instead of testing one variant at a time, define a conversion metric and let the agent generate and evaluate variants in simulation before live deployment.
  • Ad copy optimization — CTR or ROAS as the metric, copy parameters as the editable file. Runs on historical data before spend.
  • Recommendation system tuning — Ranking weights, diversity penalties, freshness decay. Engagement metric as the signal.
  • Pricing model calibration — Elasticity parameters, discount thresholds. Revenue per user as the metric.
ML & AI Systems
  • Hyperparameter tuning — The original use case. Any training script with a validation metric is fair game.
  • Prompt optimization — Treat your system prompt like
    train.py
    . Define an eval metric, let the agent iterate overnight.
  • RAG pipeline tuning — Chunk size, overlap, retrieval k, reranking thresholds. Hundreds of combinations, one retrieval quality score.
  • Inference optimization — Quantization settings, batching configs, kernel selections. Metric: throughput per dollar.
The common thread across all of these: a search space too large for manual exploration, a metric that's honest and fast to compute, and a baseline that's "good enough" because nobody had time to do better. That's the only prerequisite autoresearch needs.

How to Actually Run It: Complete Beginner Setup

The Github can be found
here
What You Need
  • An NVIDIA GPU (tested on H100, but community forks exist for consumer hardware)
  • Python 3.10+
  • uv (a fast Python package manager, far less painful than pip)
  • An AI agent like Claude or Codex to point at the repo
No big GPU? Community forks built for smaller hardware:
  • MacOS (Apple Silicon):
    miolini/autoresearch-macos
    or
    trevin-creator/autoresearch-mlx
  • Windows with consumer GPU:
    jsegov/autoresearch-win-rtx
Step 1 — Install the package manager
bash
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
Step 2 — Clone the repo and install dependencies
bash
git clone https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch
cd autoresearch
uv sync
Step 3 — Prepare the data (once, ~2 minutes)
bash
uv run prepare.py
Step 4 — Verify your setup
bash
uv run train.py
This manually runs one 5-minute experiment. If it completes and shows you a val_bpb number at the end, you're good. That number is your baseline, the score the agent will try to beat.
Step 5 — Start the agent
Open Claude Code, Codex, or whichever agent you're using. Point it at the repo directory and send this:
plaintext
Hi, have a look at program.md and let's kick off a new experiment! Let's do the setup first.
The agent reads program.md, forms a hypothesis, edits
train.py
, runs training, checks the result, commits or reverts, and loops.
You can walk away.

Running on Smaller Hardware?

Lower these settings before you start:
In
train.py
:
  • DEPTH: Change from 8 to 4. This is the primary knob for model complexity.
  • WINDOW_PATTERN: Change from "SSSL" to "L". The default pattern uses attention types that are slow on smaller GPUs.
  • TOTAL_BATCH_SIZE: Lower to 2**14 (~16,000 tokens per step).
In
prepare.py
:
  • MAX_SEQ_LEN: Lower to 256 or 512. This reduces how many tokens the model processes at once.
  • EVAL_TOKENS: Lower so validation runs faster.
Dataset swap: Use the
TinyStories dataset
instead of general web text. It's GPT-4-generated short stories with far lower entropy. Even a small model produces results that feel meaningful rather than gibberish.

The Bigger Picture: This Isn't Just About Neural Networks

Once you understand the loop, modify something, measure it, keep the wins, discard the losses, repeat, you start seeing it everywhere.
Any problem with these three properties is in scope:
  • A single honest metric you can compute automatically. val_bpb here. But it could be portfolio Sharpe ratio, compiler output speed, drug binding affinity, website conversion rate, API latency. If you can reduce "is this better?" to a number, you can automate the loop.
  • A file or configuration an agent can
    modify.Training
    code here. But it could be a trading algorithm, a compiler pass, a smart contract, a prompt template, a molecular structure in code form.
  • Experiments that run in minutes, not weeks.The faster your feedback loop, the more experiments you can run, the faster you converge. The 5-minute budget is a feature, not a constraint.
If your problem fits those three boxes, and more problems fit than you'd expect, autoresearch is the template.

The Honest Part

⚠️ Let's not oversell this.
Autoresearch is not discovering new scientific principles. It's not reading papers and synthesizing novel architectures. It's not making the kind of intuitive leap that produces a transformer or a diffusion model.
What it does is systematically close the gap between "good enough" and "actually optimal" in a search space too large for humans to cover manually.
That gap turned out to be worth 11% on a project Karpathy already thought was well-tuned. Karpathy himself said this isn't "novel, ground-breaking research (yet)." The "yet" is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
Round 2 is already running. He's also looking at how multiple agents can collaborate in parallel — dividing the search space, sharing results, promoting the most promising experiments to larger scales.
And it's not another OpenClaw.
OpenClaw is a general-purpose task executor. You give it jobs, it does them, reports back. The human stays in the decision loop even if asynchronously.
Autoresearch is a research optimizer. You set a goal metric once and walk away. No conversation, no task queue, no back-and-forth. It's pure autonomous iteration toward a measurable target. They're complementary, you could use OpenClaw to kick off autoresearch sessions, monitor git commits, and message you when a round completes.

The Verdict

The research process that made Karpathy one of the best in the world, hypothesize, implement, test, iterate, can now run without him. It doesn't replace his vision or his ability to read the field. But it handles the unglamorous tuning work that consumes most of a researcher's actual time.
For you: the question is whether your problem has an autoresearch-shaped hole in it. A metric you trust, a file an agent can edit, a fast feedback loop. If that exists in your domain, you can build this.
That's the new research skill. Not writing the code. Writing the instructions for the thing that writes the code.
The part that stays human is getting smaller with each round.
